content:
The asynctimerchan GODEBUG disappears in 1.27. Time will always use unbuffered channels. If your code relies on the buffered behavior, your window is closing.

6 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by Danial Igdery on UnsplashThe duplicate retry wasn’t a race. That was the first thing we ruled out.
Our retry helper — a small internal package wrapping time.Timer for exponential backoff — had been stable for two years across nineteen services. No incidents tied to it. No flaky tests. When a billing worker started firing a second retry attempt on roughly 1 in 4,300 requests under load, three engineers spent four days assuming the bug was in the caller, because the timer code hadn't changed.
It hadn’t. The go.mod around it had.
That’s the part that still bothers me. We audited timer usage after the Go 1.23 release. We read the release notes. We even ran bisect against a few suspect tests. And we still missed this, because the change doesn't live in the package that broke — it lives in a directive three repositories away.
Why We Believed the Timer Was Safe Everywhere
Go 1.23 made timer and ticker channels synchronous — unbuffered, capacity zero, instead of the old…
